samples.explanation: Rule 4 is an exact duplicate of rule 3, meaning it will never be evaluated since rule 3 always matches first; this is purely cosmetic with no functional impact on traffic flow or security posture.
samples.suggested_fix: Remove the duplicate rule at index 4 from the INPUT chain to keep the ruleset clean and avoid confusion during future audits.

samples.explanation: Rule 3 unconditionally accepts all TCP port 22 traffic before rule 6 can drop packets from the 10.1.0.0/16 subnet, meaning hosts in that subnet can still reach SSH despite the apparent intent to block them. This creates an unintended security gap where a network segment that should be denied access retains SSH connectivity.
samples.suggested_fix: Insert an explicit DROP rule for TCP port 22 from the 10.1.0.0/16 source (or any source range meant to be blocked) at an index before rule 3, so the deny is evaluated first; alternatively, reorder rule 6 to appear before rule 3 if the intent is to block that subnet from all services including SSH.

samples.explanation: Rule 4 accepts all TCP port 22 traffic regardless of source, and rule 6 drops all traffic from 10.1.0.0/16 regardless of protocol or port. Because rule 4 appears first, any SSH connection from 10.1.0.0/16 will be accepted and never reach rule 6, meaning the DROP for that subnet is partially bypassed for port 22. This creates ambiguity about whether internal 10.1.0.0/16 hosts should be permitted SSH access.
samples.suggested_fix: If 10.1.0.0/16 should not have SSH access, insert a source-specific DROP for tcp/22 from that subnet before rule 4 (lower index). If SSH from that subnet is intentional, document it explicitly and tighten rule 6 to exclude port 22 to avoid confusion.

samples.explanation: Rule 5 accepts all traffic from the entire 10.0.0.0/8 range, which completely shadows rule 6's intended DROP for the 10.1.0.0/16 subnet. This means traffic from 10.1.0.0/16 that was supposed to be blocked is silently accepted, defeating an explicit security boundary.
samples.suggested_fix: Move the more-specific DROP rule for 10.1.0.0/16 to an index before the broader 10.0.0.0/8 ACCEPT rule so it is evaluated first and can take effect.

samples.explanation: Rule 5 unconditionally ACCEPTs all traffic from the 10.0.0.0/8 internal range, meaning any internal host connecting to TCP/UDP ports 85-95 will be accepted by rule 5 before rule 8 (DROP) is ever evaluated. Conversely, external hosts hitting ports 85-95 are dropped correctly. This creates an ambiguous security boundary where the intended block on ports 85-95 is silently bypassed for the entire internal /8 network.
samples.suggested_fix: If ports 85-95 should be blocked even for internal hosts, insert a DROP rule for that port range above rule 5 (before index 5) so it is evaluated first; if internal hosts should be exempt from that block, document the intent explicitly and consider scoping rule 8 to non-RFC1918 sources to eliminate the ambiguity.
